metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider splitting the leaf into two sequenced child tickets — Slice 1 (singleton finder + coverage contract + gold corpus + CLI, closed on exit-0 baseline) and Slice 2 (packed batching + focused verifier + decide + verdict wiring, closed after comparative regression passes) — so the evidence gate between them is enforced by ticket dependency rather than convention.
evidence: 'First vertical slice: implement only the coverage contract, focused finder prompt, singleton runner, gold corpus, and the opt-in CLI... Do not continue until singleton recall is at least 0.90...'
'Second vertical slice: implement packed finder calls... Only after that gate passes implement focused Pass 2, separate-domain decide merging, and terminal verdict enforcement.'
The first-slice proving command exits 0 and records metrics before Slice 2 begins — these are independently verifiable delivery points.

Components touched: passes.py, sizing.py, parity.py, fidelity_spot_eval.py, verify_models.py, plan_review_decide, orchestrator.finalize_verdict, plan-review.yaml, two .md prompt files, plan_review_decide_input.schema.json, runners.py, interpreter.py, plus four distinct test suites
findings.finding: The plan's own 'Binding implementation sequence' defines two independently evidence-gated vertical slices — Slice 1 (singleton finder + coverage contract + gold corpus + CLI, gated on recall ≥ 0.90 and false-accept ≤ 0.10) and Slice 2 (packed batching + focused verifier + decide merging + verdict wiring) — each producing a named, provable artifact (exit-0 CLI command, committed baseline JSON). These satisfy the G5 decomposition test: each slice is independently testable, releasable, and valuable on its own terms, and the plan's evidence gate between them already enforces the ordering. Bundling both slices into one leaf ticket creates a session scope that spans ~15 components across heterogeneous subsystems (workflow engine interpreter, sizing infrastructure, parity tooling, verdict assembly, observability events, two new prompt files, schema updates, and four test suites). While all pieces share the single concern of prerequisite consistency, the diffusion across unrelated subsystems and the plan-defined evidence gate between slices are signals that this may be more tractable as two sequenced child tickets.
findings.location: Binding implementation sequence, steps 1–2; Implementation section overall
scenarios: A single implementer session attempting both slices risks stalling on Slice 2 decide/verdict complexity before the Slice 1 quality gate has been passed, defeating the evidence-gating intent.
If the Slice 1 quality gate is not met (recall < 0.90), Slice 2 work is wasted — the plan itself acknowledges this but combines them into one deliverable anyway.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that each finding entry passed into a split half-call carries an explicit `original_index` label (its position in the authoritative flat 0..P-1 domain), and that the verifier prompt instructions require echoing `original_index` as the emitted `index` field — so re-assembled partial results from split calls align correctly with the flat domain before attribution validation.
evidence: 'It emits the existing {index, severity_attributes, binary} entry' — the verifier output is index-keyed, but the input specification for split half-calls does not mention passing original flat indices as explicit labels

'Flatten focused findings in stable (prerequisite_id, finding_index) order into local 0..P-1 and verify only that list' — the flat index is the authoritative domain key, but split calls receive a positional subset
General verifier path relies on array position; the split recovery must override this with explicit original index labels but the plan is silent on how
findings.finding: When an oversized single verifier record's findings tuple is split into contiguous halves for two separate verifier calls, the plan does not specify that original flat 0..P-1 indices must be explicitly included as labeled inputs in each half-call so the verifier can echo them back correctly. Without explicit index labels in the prompt input, the verifier will emit position-relative indices (0-based within each half-call), making it impossible to reassemble partial results back into the authoritative flat domain — causing every second-half finding to appear out-of-range or mismatched, which the decide path converts to indeterminate/attribution-invalid, silently discarding valid focused findings.
findings.location: Binding focused prompt and failure contracts — 'split only that record's findings tuple into stable contiguous halves while repeating the complete subject plan and complete prerequisite plan text in each call; never split either plan text'
scenarios: A prerequisite with many high-severity findings causes a context-limit error; the findings tuple is split; the verifier for the second half emits indices 0..N/2-1 instead of N/2..N-1; decide treats them as duplicate or out-of-range and converts the entire prerequisite record to indeterminate/attribution-invalid
Even if an implementation workaround is applied ad hoc, the plan's omission leaves the mechanism under-specified for implementers, risking divergent solutions
findings.suggested_fix: In the verifier input schema and prompt instructions, add a required `original_index` field per finding entry. The runner constructs split half-calls by slicing the findings list and setting each entry's `original_index` to its position in the full flat list. The verifier is instructed to echo `original_index` as `index` in its output. Re-assembly then merges results by `index` before the attribution veto check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the parallel decide pipeline conflict: either (a) realign the leaf to use a single Pass 3 that handles both general and prerequisite findings with `prerequisite_id` preserved (as the epic implies), or (b) update the epic first to authorize two independently decided domains and re-certify the parent before proceeding.
evidence: Epic (jira-reb-1160) Settled Design, Prerequisite consistency review: 'No parallel decide pipeline is introduced.'

Epic: 'Pass 3 preserves prerequisite_id on upheld findings, drops attribution=no candidates as unsupported, and maps missing/malformed verification to the unsigned INDETERMINATE coverage record.' — singular Pass 3 handling both.

Leaf: 'independently reshape and decide each domain... both decide calls... Concatenate general_decided + focused_decided only after both decide calls return, then invoke the existing single partition_findings call' — two separate decide calls constitute a parallel pipeline.
Acceptance Criteria line: 'General and focused index domains are independently reshaped and cannot shift each other.' — confirms bifurcated pipeline.
findings.finding: The leaf introduces two independently decided domains (general 0..G-1 and focused 0..P-1) with separate `pass3_over_findings` calls, independent reshaping, and post-hoc concatenation — a parallel decide pipeline — directly contradicting the epic's explicit statement 'No parallel decide pipeline is introduced.'
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Preserve two non-overlapping verification index domains... independently reshape and decide each domain... Run the general path unchanged. For the focused domain... Concatenate general_decided + focused_decided only after both decide calls return'
scenarios: Implementer builds a bifurcated decide path as specified in the leaf, but this contradicts the parent's explicit design prohibition — the parent's architecture assumed a single Pass 3 that handles all findings including prerequisite ones with prerequisite_id preserved.
A later sibling story (e.g., surfaces/cb6b) or downstream consumer relies on the epic's single-pipeline assumption, creating an integration conflict.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the DET-blocker precedence conflict: either (a) align the leaf to the epic's rule (any indeterminate coverage → unsigned INDETERMINATE, regardless of DET blockers), or (b) update the parent epic's settled design and AC to authorize DET-blocker precedence over prerequisite indeterminacy, re-certify the parent, and then re-review this leaf.
evidence: Epic (jira-reb-1160) Settled Design: 'any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review rather than signed PASS' — stated without qualification or exception for DET blockers.
Epic AC: 'Every readable prerequisite receives one three-disposition coverage record' — implies INDETERMINATE on any indeterminate, not a BLOCK override.
Leaf explicitly acknowledges the conflict: 'This qualifies the epic's shorthand that any indeterminate prerequisite forces INDETERMINATE' — acknowledging divergence rather than realigning.

Leaf Binding review corrections: 'Verdict precedence is authoritative: retained DET blockers yield unsigned BLOCK even when prerequisite coverage is indeterminate' — the leaf claims its own rule is authoritative over the parent's.
findings.finding: The leaf's DET-blocker/INDETERMINATE verdict precedence rule ('a concurrent DET blocker forces unsigned BLOCK while preserving coverage.prerequisite_indeterminate=true') directly contradicts the parent epic's unqualified rule 'any indeterminate record yields an unsigned INDETERMINATE review.' The leaf itself acknowledges this is a 'qualification' of the parent's stated rule.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'an indeterminate prerequisite with no DET blocker forces top-level unsigned INDETERMINATE; a concurrent DET blocker forces unsigned BLOCK while preserving coverage.prerequisite_indeterminate=true'; AC: 'An indeterminate prerequisite with no DET blocker forces top-level unsigned INDETERMINATE; a concurrent DET blocker forces unsigned BLOCK'
scenarios: A scenario with both a DET structural blocker and an indeterminate prerequisite record: the epic mandates INDETERMINATE, the leaf mandates BLOCK. These yield different verdicts, creating a downstream consumer inconsistency.
An agent relying on the epic's rule would expect INDETERMINATE and trigger re-review; the leaf's rule yields BLOCK and triggers a different remediation path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the complete data-flow path for `prerequisite_coverage` from the `finders` batch step output through `plan_review_decide` output schema and into the `plan_review_coach` step input, naming the exact YAML `with:` or step-output reference used in each of the four call sites.
evidence: Current `plan_review_coach` in `workflow_ops.py` lines 546-558: `coverage` is built from `det_coverage`, `routing`, `llm_ran` — no `prerequisite_coverage` input exists.

Plan: 'pass that same dictionary through the existing `coverage=coverage` argument to `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`' — but the data-flow path from `${{ steps.finders.outputs.prerequisite_coverage }}` → `plan_review_decide` output → `plan_review_coach` input is not specified in any YAML or schema block.

Plan: 'In `plan_review_coach_inputs`, compute `reclassified = [f for f in indeterminate if f.get("reason") == "prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate"]`' — but `plan_review_coach_inputs` is a DIFFERENT op from `plan_review_coach`; the plan conflates the two in different sections.
findings.finding: The plan's `plan_review_coach` modification — adding `prerequisite_indeterminate` to the `coverage` dictionary — does not specify where the `prerequisite_coverage` input arrives in `plan_review_coach`. The current `plan_review_coach` step (`workflow_ops.py`) builds `coverage` from `det_coverage`, `routing`, and `llm_ran` inputs. The plan says 'build the existing local `coverage` dictionary with a new `prerequisite_indeterminate` key computed as `any(record.disposition == "indeterminate")` from its `prerequisite_coverage` input', but `plan_review_coach` in the current workflow receives inputs from the `plan_review_decide` step's outputs — and the plan specifies four YAML call sites for `plan_review_coach_inputs` but never specifies which of those four YAML call sites passes `prerequisite_coverage` as an input, nor how `prerequisite_coverage` flows from the `finders` batch step output through `plan_review_decide` output to the `plan_review_coach` step input.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Wire focused finder and verifier steps and arrays through both branches in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml`. In `workflow_ops.plan_review_coach`, build the existing local `coverage` dictionary with a new `prerequisite_indeterminate` key…'
scenarios: An implementer updating `plan_review_coach` in `workflow_ops.py` must infer whether `prerequisite_coverage` arrives as a direct step input, is read from the store, or is passed through `plan_review_decide`'s output schema — none of these paths are specified.

If `prerequisite_coverage` is not threaded into the `plan_review_coach` YAML call site, `coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')` is always absent and the INDETERMINATE verdict branch in `finalize_verdict` is never reached from the coach path.

findings.finding: The behavioral changes to `finalize_verdict` (new INDETERMINATE branch before existing elif chain) and `plan_review_decide` (blocker-to-indeterminate reclassification when any prerequisite record is indeterminate) have no rollback or back-out mechanism described. If the focused finder/verifier route produces spurious evaluation-error indeterminate records at scale (e.g., due to provider instability), every plan review would be forced to INDETERMINATE with no way to disable the path without a code deploy.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'In plan_review_decide, after the single partition_findings call…' and 'In orchestrator.finalize_verdict, add if coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate")…'
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a configuration flag (e.g., `PREREQUISITE_COVERAGE_ENABLED: bool = True` in a feature-flags module or workflow YAML parameter) that, when false, causes `ProductionBatchRunner` to return an empty `prerequisite_coverage` array and `plan_review_decide` to skip the indeterminate-promotion logic entirely. Document the flag as the back-out path in this plan's Implementation section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'tail conflict' in the Implementation section (e.g., 'a bin where the last prerequisite block in lexicographic order is the only block in the bin because all earlier blocks filled prior bins') so that gold-set authors and reviewers can verify the fixture exercises the stated invariant.
evidence: Plan: 'Stable bins contain whole blocks only. Neither subject nor prerequisite is split. A tail conflict is evaluated identically to a singleton call.'
Plan: 'Add eight or more gold sets of at least three prerequisites: two all-consistent, two single-conflict, two tail-conflict, one attribution-confusion, and one multi-bin.'
The term 'tail conflict' does not appear anywhere in the existing codebase (`src/`, `docs/`, `tests/`) — it is not a term of art established elsewhere in this codebase.
No definition of 'tail conflict' appears in the 'Binding criterion and CLI registration', 'Binding implementation sequence', 'Binding signal-chain', or any other section of the plan.
findings.finding: The term 'tail conflict' is used normatively in two places — 'tail-conflict fixtures' in the batching rule and 'two tail-conflict gold sets' in the fidelity spec — but is never defined anywhere in the plan. An executor who must write the gold sets and the tail-conflict batching fixture cannot determine what constitutes a 'tail conflict' scenario without reverse-engineering it from context.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Keep batching semantically neutral… A tail conflict is evaluated identically to a singleton call'; Implementation section — gold corpus spec 'two tail-conflict'
scenarios: An implementer writing `prerequisite_packing_spot_eval` gold fixtures cannot determine what the two 'tail-conflict' gold sets should contain — whether they test a bin whose last block barely fits, an edge case where the last block in lexicographic order conflicts, or something else.
A PR reviewer cannot verify that the tail-conflict fixture tests the intended property without a definition.
The fidelity gate can silently pass with tail-conflict test cases that do not actually exercise the intended edge case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact Pydantic model for the `Finding` type used inside `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord.findings`, specifying whether it extends the existing `P1Finding` model or is a new class, and explicitly listing all required fields including `prerequisite_id`.
evidence: Plan: 'outer `PrerequisiteCoverageOutput { records: list[PrerequisiteCoverageRecord] }`. Each record is `{prerequisite_id: canonical string, disposition: consistent|finding|indeterminate, findings: list[Finding], reason_code?: ..., detail?: string}`'

Plan: 'each finding uses the existing Pass-1 finding fields `finding`, `criteria`, `location`, `evidence`, `scenarios`, `impact`, `checklist_item`, and `suggested_fix`, plus required `prerequisite_id`'
The existing `P1Finding` model in `passes.py` does not have a `prerequisite_id` field — so `Finding` here cannot be the existing type verbatim; the plan never names the new model explicitly.
No `plan_review_prerequisite_finder.md` exists in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` — the prompt that would carry the schema is not yet created, so the type definition is floating.
findings.finding: The `Finding` type referenced inside `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord.findings: list[Finding]` is never defined in the plan's schema specification. The plan defines `PrerequisiteCoverageOutput { records: list[PrerequisiteCoverageRecord] }` and specifies each `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` has a `findings: list[Finding]` field, but never defines what `Finding` is in this context — whether it is the existing `P1Finding` Pydantic class from `passes.py`, a new prerequisite-scoped subclass, or a plain dict with specific keys. The finder prompt section separately lists the per-finding fields (`finding`, `criteria`, `location`, `evidence`, `scenarios`, `impact`, `checklist_item`, `suggested_fix`, `prerequisite_id`), but the plan never reconciles these field enumerations with the `Finding` type named in the schema model definition.
findings.location: Implementation section — `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` schema definition; Binding focused prompt and failure contracts — finder output fields
scenarios: An implementer building `_prerequisite_coverage_model()` must choose whether `Finding` is a new Pydantic model, the existing `P1Finding` extended with `prerequisite_id`, or a plain dict — the plan leaves this ambiguous.
Contract validation code that checks `records[i].findings[j].prerequisite_id` will fail or be wrong depending on which interpretation is used.
The `contracts.register_contract('plan_review_prerequisite_coverage', _prerequisite_coverage_model)` call produces a model that cannot be validated without knowing the `Finding` type.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'Define `PrerequisiteFinderFinding` as a Pydantic model with the following fields: `finding: str`, `criteria: list[str]` (fixed to `["prerequisite-consistency"]`), `location: str = ""`, `evidence: list[str] = []`, `scenarios: list[str] = []`, `impact: str = ""`, `checklist_item: str = ""`, `suggested_fix: str = ""`, `prerequisite_id: str` (required, equals the enclosing record ID). Use this as the `Finding` type in `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named unit test (e.g., `test_mint_finding_id_different_prerequisite_ids_produce_distinct_ids` in `tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py`) and reference it in the Verification section so AC10 has a concrete proving command.
evidence: The Binding signal-chain section defines the canonical four-field encoding and states 'identical text from different prerequisites cannot deduplicate.'
No test name containing 'mint_finding_id' or 'distinct_finding_id' or 'same_text' appears in any test file listed in the Verification section.
The Verification pytest commands do not include a `-k` selector or named test for this behavior.
findings.finding: AC10 ('Same-text findings from different prerequisite IDs retain distinct finding IDs') has no named test or proving command that maps to it in the Verification section, leaving the criterion unverified by any described step.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Same-text findings from different prerequisite IDs retain distinct finding IDs.'; Binding signal-chain and identity tests section.
scenarios: A regression in `mint_finding_id` causes same-text findings from two prerequisites to share an ID, silently deduplicating findings; no specified command would catch it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Binding signal-chain and identity tests: 'Add `test_mint_finding_id_different_prerequisite_ids_produce_distinct_ids` asserting that two calls to `mint_finding_id` with identical finding text and criteria but different `prerequisite_id` values return non-equal ID strings.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name a test (e.g., `test_indeterminate_record_has_no_findings` in `tests/unit/test_plan_review_prerequisites.py`) or explicitly state that the Pydantic schema for `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` enforces `findings=[]` when `disposition='indeterminate'` and reference its schema-validation test as the proving command for AC11.
evidence: The Implementation section specifies: 'indeterminate requires no findings and one closed-enum reason code.'
No test name asserting the absence of findings on indeterminate records is listed in any test file in the Verification section.

The schema contract could enforce this if the `PrerequisiteCoverageRecord` Pydantic model validates it, but the plan does not explicitly state this validation is schema-enforced vs. behaviorally tested.
findings.finding: AC11 ('Every indeterminate record contains no incompatibility finding') has no named test or proving command in the Verification section that specifically asserts this structural constraint.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Every indeterminate record contains no incompatibility finding.'
scenarios: A code path that creates an indeterminate record while accidentally retaining a findings list passes all other tests but violates this AC, allowing a misleading record to reach the coach.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the required front-matter fields for both `plan_review_prerequisite_finder.md` and `plan_review_prerequisite_verifier.md` (including `id:`, `category:`, and `output_schema:` values) in the plan, so the prompt-library machinery can resolve and contract-bind them.
evidence: Confirmed: no file matching `plan_review_prerequisite_finder.md` or `plan_review_prerequisite_verifier.md` exists in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` (directory listing shows all existing reviewer files).
Existing reviewer files (e.g., `plan_review_finder.md`, `plan_review_verifier.md`) use front-matter with `id:`, `category:`, `output_schema:` fields parsed by `prompts.get_prompt()`.

Plan specifies only the system instruction content ('compare the subject plan independently with each supplied prerequisite plan…') but not the YAML/TOML front-matter fields the prompt-library machinery requires.
PASS_PREREQUISITE_FINDER = 'plan-review-prerequisite-finder' and PASS_PREREQUISITE_VERIFIER = 'plan-review-prerequisite-verifier' are declared but neither prompt file exists to be resolved.
findings.finding: The two prompt files the plan mandates — `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_prerequisite_finder.md` (resolved as `plan-review-prerequisite-finder`) and `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_prerequisite_verifier.md` (resolved as `plan-review-prerequisite-verifier`) — do not exist in the repository, and the plan names them as files to create but does not define the full system-prompt text or the front-matter contract fields (e.g., `output_schema:`, `category:`, `id:`) that every other reviewer file in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` carries. The plan specifies the *instructions* these prompts should give the model but not the prompt-library front-matter that `prompts.get_prompt()` and the `da27` prompt machinery require to register and resolve the prompt.
findings.location: Binding focused prompt and failure contracts — 'Create `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_prerequisite_finder.md`…'; 'Create `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_prerequisite_verifier.md`…'
scenarios: When `prompts.get_prompt('plan-review-prerequisite-finder', repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` is called, it will fail because the file does not exist and the plan does not specify the fallback.

The front-matter `output_schema:` field that links the prompt to the `plan_review_prerequisite_coverage` contract is never specified, so the prompt-library machinery cannot enforce the output contract at the prompt level.

evidence: description is 31442 chars (> 24000)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all four `plan_review_coach_inputs` `with:` blocks in `plan-review.yaml` as edit targets that must add `indeterminate: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.indeterminate }}` to supply the `reclassified` bucket.
evidence: Plan: 'In plan_review_coach_inputs, compute reclassified = [f for f in indeterminate if f.get("reason") == "prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate"]'
Current plan-review.yaml coach_inputs with: blocks (lines 179-183 and 259-263) pass ticket_id, surviving, blocking — not indeterminate
plan_review_coach_inputs at workflow_ops.py:392 reads ctx.inputs.get('surviving') and ctx.inputs.get('blocking') — indeterminate is not currently wired
findings.finding: The plan adds `reclassified` indeterminate findings to `coachable` in `plan_review_coach_inputs`, but does not enumerate that all four `coach_inputs` step wiring sites in `plan-review.yaml` must also pass `indeterminate: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.indeterminate }}` as a new `with:` input — otherwise `ctx.inputs.get('indeterminate')` in `plan_review_coach_inputs` will always be empty.
findings.location: Binding workflow production section + src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml (four coach_inputs with: blocks)
scenarios: At runtime, reclassified is always empty because indeterminate is never passed through the YAML wiring, silently defeating the coaching-for-reclassified-blockers requirement
findings.suggested_fix: Add `indeterminate: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.indeterminate }}` to all four `plan_review_coach_inputs` `with:` blocks in `plan-review.yaml`, and update the file_impact list to include this specific change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan or expand-contract sequence for the `plan_review_decide` non-DET-blocker demotion and `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` INDETERMINATE-branch insertion, e.g., a `prerequisite_routing_enabled` feature flag that short-circuits both mutations and restores prior behavior without a code change.
evidence: Plan asserts correctness via tests and call-site analysis ('coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate") is false on those paths') but names no feature flag, no expand-contract sequence, and no back-out procedure for the finalizer and decide mutations.

'In plan_review_decide, after the single partition_findings call, if any final prerequisite coverage record is indeterminate, retain every tier == "DET" entry in parts["blocking"]; move only non-DET blockers into parts["indeterminate"]' — this mutates existing decide logic with no reversibility mechanism stated.
T4 rubric requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — acknowledgement alone is insufficient.
findings.finding: The changes to `plan_review_decide` (moving non-DET blockers to `parts['indeterminate']`) and `orchestrator.finalize_verdict` (inserting a new INDETERMINATE branch) are substantive behavioral modifications to shared production paths. The plan acknowledges safe call-site interaction but provides no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing if these changes introduce regressions in production.
findings.location: Implementation — 'This story explicitly modifies both decision assembly and terminal verdict' and 'Binding comparative baseline and compatibility behavior — verdict arrays and string remain consistent'
scenarios: If the indeterminate prerequisite detection produces false positives in production (e.g., snapshot preload returns spurious indeterminate records due to upstream bugs), non-DET blockers are silently demoted to indeterminate with no way to disable the new path without a code deploy.
A regression in finalizer logic affects all plan-review verdicts, not just prerequisite-involving ones, and there is no feature flag to revert to the prior behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a single boolean gate (e.g., `PREREQUISITE_ROUTING_ENABLED` env var or config flag, defaulting true) checked at the top of both the decide mutation block and the finalizer's new INDETERMINATE branch. When false, both functions skip the new logic entirely and produce byte-identical output to the pre-story behavior. Document the flag and its rollback procedure in a brief ops note or AGENTS.md section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate all four `plan_review_decide` `with:` blocks in `plan-review.yaml` as edit targets requiring `has_prerequisites`, `prerequisite_coverage`, `prerequisite_findings`, and `prerequisite_verifications` inputs wired from `steps.finders.outputs.*`.
evidence: Plan: 'Add optional has_prerequisites, prerequisite_coverage, prerequisite_findings, and prerequisite_verifications properties to plan_review_decide_input.schema.json; Both YAML branches always pass false and empty arrays when the route does not apply.'
Current plan-review.yaml decide with: blocks at lines 171-175 and 250-255 pass findings, verifications, det_blocking, det_advisory only
Plan says 'Every downstream branch references ${{ steps.finders.outputs.prerequisite_coverage }}' but BatchRunResult in runners.py currently only returns findings, criteria_count, batch_plan
findings.finding: The plan requires passing `prerequisite_coverage`, `prerequisite_findings`, `prerequisite_verifications`, and `has_prerequisites` into `plan_review_decide` via the YAML `with:` blocks, but does not enumerate that all four `plan_review_decide` `with:` call sites in `plan-review.yaml` (the agentic-verifier arm and the single-turn-verifier arm) must be updated — and that the `${{ steps.finders.outputs.prerequisite_coverage }}` expression must actually be produced by the extended batch runner.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Add optional has_prerequisites...') + src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml (four decide with: blocks at lines 171-175 and 250-255)
scenarios: Without explicit enumeration of all four decide with: sites, one arm could be missed, leaving prerequisite coverage silently absent from one verifier path

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a wall-clock timeout (or maximum total-elapsed-time budget) for the complete focused prerequisite review sub-flow — finder + verifier + escalation — and specify what disposition is emitted when the budget is exceeded (e.g., indeterminate/evaluation-error with detail=timeout).
evidence: The plan specifies retry/backoff and model escalation but never states a maximum wall-clock duration for the focused prerequisite sub-flow.
No AC or done-definition includes a latency bound for the focused path.
The plan-review gate is user-facing (blocks or unblocks ticket progression).
Model escalation can chain multiple slow LLM calls before reaching indeterminate/evaluation-error.
findings.finding: The focused prerequisite review sub-flow (finder bins → verifier bins → decide → verdict) has no wall-clock timeout or latency target. For a blocking plan-review gate, unbounded elapsed time is a MAJOR gap per T5a: 'a user-facing operation with no latency target is MAJOR.'
findings.location: Implementation — 'Focused finder and verifier calls use the existing runner retry classification and exponential-backoff policy'; Binding operational behavior
scenarios: A prerequisite block that repeatedly hits context limits escalates through every model in models_at_or_above() with no timeout; the review gate stalls for minutes.
Transient provider slowness causes repeated retry cycles with exponential backoff across multiple bins, holding the gate open indefinitely.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a per-review `prerequisite_review_timeout_seconds` config value (e.g., 120 s). Thread a deadline into the focused runner; any operation that would exceed the deadline short-circuits remaining bins to `indeterminate/evaluation-error` with `detail=timeout`. Add this to ACs and the observability event.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a maximum supported prerequisite count per review run (or a maximum focused-finder bin count) and specify what happens when exceeded (e.g., truncate with a warning, or emit indeterminate for excess prerequisites), so per-review cost is bounded.
evidence: pack_prerequisite_bins packs greedily but produces as many bins as needed for all prerequisites — no stated maximum bin count or prerequisite count.
pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins similarly produces O(N) bins.
No AC or Implementation section states a maximum number of prerequisites or a maximum number of LLM calls per review run.
No per-review cost ceiling or circuit-breaker on total call count is mentioned.
findings.finding: The plan imposes no upper bound on the number of prerequisites per ticket, meaning the focused finder and verifier together issue O(N_prerequisites) LLM calls per review run with no stated cap. At unconstrained N this is an unbounded fan-out with proportional cost and no economic ceiling.
findings.location: Implementation — pack_prerequisite_bins, pack_prerequisite_verifier_bins; Binding criterion and CLI registration
scenarios: A ticket with 30 direct prerequisites (e.g., a large integration story) that all fail singleton packing generates 30 finder calls and 30 verifier calls — 60+ LLM calls per review, with no economic guard.
A misconfigured dependency graph with cycles or duplicates (despite deduplication) inflates call counts before deduplication takes effect.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a config constant `MAX_PREREQUISITE_BINS = N` (e.g., 10). When `pack_prerequisite_bins` would produce more than N bins, log a warning, cap to the first N bins in stable order, and emit `indeterminate/evaluation-error` with `detail=prerequisite-count-exceeded` for remaining IDs. Document the cap in ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `with_inputs: Mapping[str, Any]` is truly needed as a generic field or whether a specifically-typed `relation_snapshot: Any = None` field on `BatchRunRequest` (or a typed subclass/protocol) would serve the single current consumer while preserving the all-named-fields design.
evidence: Plan: 'Extend `BatchRunRequest` in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/runners.py` with `with_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)`'

Codebase: `BatchRunRequest` currently has 9 specifically-named typed fields (`finder`, `criteria`, `usd_budget`, `model_ladder`, `workflow`, `target_ticket`, `repo_root`, `run_id`, `step_id`); all are concrete and named
Plan: 'ProductionBatchRunner reads `req.with_inputs["relation_snapshot"]`' — exactly one key is ever placed in this generic map
Rule-of-Three: only one current call site supplies a `with:` block containing `relation_snapshot`
findings.finding: The plan extends `BatchRunRequest` with a generic `with_inputs: Mapping[str, Any]` field to pass a single named value (`relation_snapshot`) from the YAML `with:` block to `ProductionBatchRunner`. Every other field on `BatchRunRequest` is a specifically-typed, named field; adding an untyped generic escape hatch for exactly one concrete use-case violates both Rule-of-Three (one real consumer) and introduces config-surface proliferation by creating an unstructured channel future stories can exploit without going through the typed-field design review cycle.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend `BatchRunRequest` in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/runners.py` with `with_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)`'
scenarios: A future story adds a second `with:` key (e.g. `execution_context`) without a typed field review, proliferating the untyped channel
A typo in the key string (`'relation_snapshott'`) silently produces `None` instead of a `KeyError`, breaking the prerequisite route with no type-checker warning
findings.suggested_fix: Add a named field `relation_snapshot: object = None` to `BatchRunRequest` instead of a generic mapping; update `_run_batch` to populate it from `step.get('with', {}).get('relation_snapshot')`; update `ProductionBatchRunner` to read `req.relation_snapshot`. This preserves the typed-field contract, avoids an unstructured escape hatch, and is byte-identical for every existing call site that omits `with:`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether `plan_review_verification_model` can add `prerequisite_attribution_valid` by extending the already-built `Binary` type directly (e.g., subclassing or creating a derived model after `_build_binary` returns) rather than adding a generic `extra_fields` parameter to the shared builder.
evidence: Plan: 'Change `_build_binary(forbid, *, extra_fields: dict[str, tuple[type, Any]] | None = None) -> type`; after building existing fields, merge `extra_fields or {}` into the `create_model` field mapping'
Plan: 'Only `plan_review_verification_model` passes `binary_extra_fields=...'`; 'Base and code-review callers omit the keyword and remain byte-identical'
Codebase: `_build_binary` has two call sites via `_build_verification_output` (line 115 in verify_models.py); Rule-of-Three for `extra_fields` usage: one consumer
findings.finding: The plan extends the shared private builder `_build_binary` with a new `extra_fields: dict[str, tuple[type, Any]] | None = None` keyword argument used by exactly ONE of its three callers (`plan_review_verification_model`). The other two callers (`verification_model`, `code_review_verification_model`) omit the keyword and remain byte-identical. Adding a generic extension point to a shared private builder for a single consumer is premature abstraction; the one extra field (`prerequisite_attribution_valid`) could be added directly in `plan_review_verification_model` after receiving the `Binary` type from `_build_binary`, without touching the shared builder at all.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Change `_build_binary(forbid, *, extra_fields: dict[str, tuple[type, Any]] | None = None) -> type`'; file `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py`
scenarios: The generic `extra_fields` mechanism invites future stories to add arbitrary binary sub-questions to any verification model through this backdoor, bypassing the contract review that a named field addition would trigger
The `extra_fields` dict's `dict[str, tuple[type, Any]]` typing is less readable than a named field and gives no IDE autocomplete for the field name or type

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py` to the plan's file_impact with reason 'Extend finalize_verdict to check coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate") and produce INDETERMINATE verdict before remaining verdict branches.'
evidence: Plan: 'In orchestrator.finalize_verdict, deterministic blockers dominate: if blocking is non-empty, verdict remains BLOCK; otherwise coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate") produces INDETERMINATE before the remaining verdict branches.'
Current finalize_verdict at orchestrator.py:519-535 does not read prerequisite_indeterminate at all

orchestrator.py is not listed in the plan's file_impact section — the plan's file impact lists passes.py, sizing.py, workflow_ops.py, plan-review.yaml, schema files, parity.py, fidelity_spot_eval.py but omits orchestrator.py
findings.finding: The plan says `plan_review_coach` should build `coverage` with a new `prerequisite_indeterminate` key and pass it to `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`. But `finalize_verdict` at `orchestrator.py:497` currently only checks `coverage.get('llm_unavailable')`, `coverage.get('verify_failed')`, and the blocking/indeterminate arrays — it does NOT check `coverage.get('prerequisite_indeterminate')`. The plan says `finalize_verdict` must be modified (`orchestrator.finalize_verdict` dominance check), but `orchestrator.py` is NOT listed in the plan's file_impact.
findings.location: Implementation section ('In orchestrator.finalize_verdict, deterministic blockers dominate...') + src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:497
scenarios: An indeterminate prerequisite with no DET blocker would produce PASS rather than INDETERMINATE because finalize_verdict never checks the coverage flag — this directly violates AC 'An indeterminate prerequisite with no DET blocker forces top-level unsigned INDETERMINATE'
findings.suggested_fix: Add orchestrator.py to file_impact: 'Extend finalize_verdict verdict logic to honor prerequisite_indeterminate coverage flag before the indeterminate-but-no-surfaced branch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the 4-byte big-endian length-prefixed binary encoding over a simpler namespace-prefixed delimiter extension of the existing `mint_finding_id` scheme, or adopt the simpler approach.
evidence: The existing scheme is described as 'pipe-delimited encoding byte-for-byte' (Binding comparative baseline). No rationale is given for why a binary length-prefixed frame is needed instead of e.g. a prefixed delimiter like `prereq:<id>|<text>|<criteria>`.

A simpler namespace-prefix approach (e.g., `prerequisite-finding-id-v1:<id>|…`) would guarantee the same cross-prerequisite non-deduplication property with far less implementation surface and no endianness dependency.
findings.finding: The plan defines a custom 4-field length-prefixed binary encoding (version string + finding text + criteria + prerequisite ID, each prefixed with a 4-byte big-endian length, then SHA-256) for prerequisite-scoped finding IDs without considering whether the existing `mint_finding_id` pipe-delimited scheme could be extended with a namespace prefix to achieve the same collision-avoidance goal. The novel binary framing is more complex to implement and audit than a simple prefix+delimiter extension, and its justification over the simpler approach is not stated.
findings.location: Binding signal-chain and identity tests — 'single canonical prerequisite-scoped identity encoding is exact: encode four fields as UTF-8, prefix each with a 4-byte big-endian unsigned byte length…'
scenarios: Implementers must correctly pack 4-byte big-endian lengths in Python, write unit tests for the exact byte layout, and keep encoder/decoder in sync — all extra surface for a property achievable with a trivial string prefix.

A bug in the length-prefix logic (e.g., off-by-one on multi-byte UTF-8 characters) silently produces wrong IDs that differ from what stored IDs expect, breaking idempotency without an obvious error signal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate whether the `relation_snapshot` can be threaded to `ProductionBatchRunner` through the existing store/expression-resolver path (as a named step output) before introducing the generic `with_inputs` field on `BatchRunRequest`.
evidence: The plan describes the seam as 'not YAML metadata ignored by the runner' and adds tests for it, implying it is a first-class workflow-engine extension.

No prior art or alternative designs (e.g., passing the snapshot as a named step output that the batch step reads from the store, which is how other cross-step data flows appear to work) are considered.
Adding `with_inputs` to `BatchRunRequest` makes arbitrary out-of-band data injectable into any future batch step — a capability whose scope is not bounded by the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new generic `with_inputs: Mapping[str, Any]` seam on `BatchRunRequest` and corresponding resolution logic in `interpreter.py::_run_batch`, treating it as an 'explicit generic workflow-engine input seam.' This is a novel workflow-engine capability with broad applicability — yet the plan does not evaluate whether an existing data-passing mechanism (e.g., step outputs already resolved through the expression resolver, or a dedicated context/store read in the runner) could serve the same purpose without widening the `BatchRunRequest` API surface permanently.
findings.location: Binding workflow production and verifier batching — 'Implement that delivery by extending `BatchRunRequest` in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/runners.py` with `with_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)`…'
scenarios: Future developers use the `with_inputs` escape hatch to bypass the normal store/expression-resolver data flow, creating two competing patterns for cross-step data passing.
The widened API makes it harder to reason about what data a batch step can receive, complicating security review and testing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit finder singleton context-limit termination rule: specify that when a single `PrerequisiteBlock` alone exceeds the largest available window, the finder emits `indeterminate/evaluation-error` with `detail=input-too-large` for that block (mirroring the verifier's authoritative rule), and document this in both the Implementation sizing section and Binding focused prompt section.
evidence: 'On context-limit error, split a stable bin into contiguous halves recursively. Escalate a singleton through models_at_or_above(). Largest-window exhaustion emits indeterminate/evaluation-error with detail=input-too-large.'
'Keep batching semantically neutral … Neither subject nor prerequisite is split.'

'Focused verifier context-limit recovery has one authoritative algorithm … convert the whole prerequisite record to indeterminate/evaluation-error with detail=input-too-large' — this is labeled authoritative for the *verifier* only.
AC 13: 'Largest-window exhaustion emits indeterminate/evaluation-error with detail=input-too-large' — does not disambiguate finder vs verifier path.
findings.finding: The finder context-limit split rule contradicts itself: Implementation states both 'split a stable bin into contiguous halves recursively' (implying recursive halving is the recovery path) and 'Neither subject nor prerequisite is split' (which makes halving impossible for a single-block bin). The Binding focused prompt section provides an explicit termination rule (`indeterminate/evaluation-error` + `detail=input-too-large`) only for the *verifier* path. The finder path for a singleton oversized block that cannot be halved is left without an authoritative resolution, sending the implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Implementation § sizing/splitting; Binding focused prompt and failure contracts § 'Focused verifier context-limit recovery'; AC line 13
scenarios: Implementer writes finder split logic that attempts recursive halving on a single-block bin, reaching a dead end because the prerequisite block cannot be split, with no specified fallback.
Implementer assumes AC 13 covers the finder singleton by analogy but the Implementation text implies recursive halving should continue, leading to an infinite loop or silent error.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, after 'Escalate a singleton through models_at_or_above()', add: 'When a single PrerequisiteBlock alone exceeds the largest-window model after escalation, emit indeterminate/evaluation-error with detail=input-too-large for that canonical ID.' Mirror this wording in the Binding focused prompt section alongside the verifier algorithm.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal AC line for observability: e.g. 'Every evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record emits a `plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` structured event containing ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size, and no prerequisite plan text; counters are partitioned by reason code.'
evidence: 'Emit structured event plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate with ticket ID, prerequisite ID, reason code, model, attempt count, and bin size for every evaluation-error, output-invalid, or attribution-invalid record. Increment counters partitioned by reason code; never include prerequisite plan text.'
'Add acceptance coverage for observability: tests assert each evaluation-error, output-invalid, and attribution-invalid record emits plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate…'
ABSENCE: No AC line names observability events, counters, or the constraint that captured events contain no prerequisite plan text.
findings.finding: Structured observability event emission (`plan_review_prerequisite_indeterminate` with required fields) and per-reason-code counters are described deliverables with no formal AC in the 14-line list.
findings.location: Binding focused prompt and failure contracts § 'Add acceptance coverage for observability'; Acceptance Criteria (none of the 14 AC lines)
scenarios: Story accepted when all 14 AC pass, but observability events are never emitted (or emit prerequisite plan text), with no formal gate to catch the regression.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section documenting the key architectural decisions introduced by this story: two-domain index management, DET-blocker-dominates-indeterminate verdict precedence, the `with:`-seam for snapshot delivery to batch runners, and the canonical four-field prerequisite finding identity encoding.
evidence: Absence: no mention of 'ADR', 'AGENTS.md', 'design doc', or 'architecture decision record' anywhere in the plan.

The plan spans changes to passes.py, sizing.py, parity.py, fidelity_spot_eval.py, verify_models.py, interpreter.py, runners.py, plan-review.yaml, coach, decide, and orchestrator — clearly crossing component boundaries and introducing a new pattern.
T5e rubric: 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update' is required when a plan introduces a new pattern/contract/pipeline stage.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel two-domain index management pattern, a new focused-prompt batching topology, a modified verdict assembly pipeline with new precedence rules, and a new criterion type — but mentions no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc addition to capture these architectural decisions durably.
findings.location: Implementation section (entire); Binding implementation sequence; Binding signal-chain and identity tests; Binding workflow production and verifier batching
scenarios: Future maintainers modifying verdict assembly or batching logic have no durable record of why two-domain index management was chosen, why DET blockers dominate indeterminate prerequisite signals, or why the `with:` YAML seam was chosen over a direct store read — leading to inadvertent regressions.
Onboarding a new contributor to the plan-review system requires reading the full ticket history rather than a design doc.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `block_threshold` values in the criterion registry are already managed as configurable entries (e.g., via a config file or environment override) consistent with existing criteria, or explicitly document that 0.60 was derived from the slice-1 baseline evaluation and is intentionally a code-level constant.
evidence: 'block_threshold: 0.60' is stated as a literal constant with no mention of how to tune it without modifying source.
The singleton evaluation (slice 1) gates recall and false-acceptance of finding detection, but does not gate or derive the block_threshold value — those are separate concerns.
T5e: 'Rules/thresholds expected to evolve are configurable, not hardcoded.'
findings.finding: The `block_threshold: 0.60` for the new `prerequisite-consistency` criterion is asserted as a fixed registry value with no stated derivation from data and no indication that it is configurable post-registration. If calibration of this threshold against observed recall/precision on the gold corpus reveals a different optimal value, changing it requires a code change rather than a configuration update.
findings.location: Binding criterion and CLI registration — 'Register criterion ID `prerequisite-consistency` with `default_posture: blocking`, `block_threshold: 0.60`'
scenarios: After deployment, observed production behavior shows 0.60 is too permissive (many false-positive blocks) or too conservative (missed incompatibilities not reaching block). Adjusting requires a code change and redeploy rather than a config update.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale statement for using positional indices in the focused verification domain (e.g., 'the certified epic contract mandates the index-keyed plan_review_verification schema; the focused domain reuses it to share the same threshold pipeline without a parallel contract') so implementers understand the constraint and can correctly handle split re-indexing.
evidence: No positive rationale is given for using positional indices rather than (prerequisite_id, finding_index) tuples as the verification key for the focused domain

'Reusing plan_review_verification is deliberate because the certified epic requires the optional attribution field in the existing index-keyed Pass-2 contract' — this justifies contract reuse but not positional indexing specifically
The split-findings recovery complexity (finding above) is a direct consequence of this design choice
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for choosing a separate two-domain index space (general 0..G-1 and focused 0..P-1) rather than an ID-keyed verification design for the focused path. Because focused findings are already keyed by `prerequisite_id`, an ID-keyed verifier contract would eliminate the index alignment and split re-indexing complexity entirely. The absence of a stated rationale for the index-based design leaves implementers without guidance on why this approach was chosen over the simpler alternative, and whether the index scheme is a hard requirement of the upstream epic contract.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Preserve two non-overlapping verification index domains. General arrays retain 0..G-1. Flatten focused findings in stable (prerequisite_id, finding_index) order into local 0..P-1 and verify only that list.'
scenarios: Implementers independently decide to use ID-keyed output for the focused verifier to simplify split recovery, diverging from the index-keyed epic contract without realizing it
The plan is read as requiring exactly positional indices, leading to the split re-indexing bug described in the companion finding
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the Implementation section: 'The focused path uses positional 0..P-1 indices rather than ID-keyed output because the certified epic requires the existing index-keyed plan_review_verification contract for the attribution veto; implementers must pass explicit original_index labels through split calls to preserve alignment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document or verify that `finalize_verdict` has no existing coverage-signal dispatch that the new `prerequisite_indeterminate` branch must integrate with, and confirm the new branch follows any established pattern for coverage-driven verdict overrides.
evidence: The plan says 'coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate")' is checked as a new branch inside `finalize_verdict` but does not describe how other existing coverage keys are handled or whether there is an existing extension point.

Binding comparative baseline notes: 'The P8 short-circuit and drift_refresh call sites pass no prerequisite coverage by design; coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate") is false on those paths' — suggesting the coverage dict already has other keys whose precedence relative to the new key is unspecified.
findings.finding: The plan adds `prerequisite_indeterminate` as a new key to an existing shared `coverage` dictionary passed to `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`, and introduces a new verdict-precedence rule (indeterminate prerequisite without DET blocker → INDETERMINATE, DET blocker present → BLOCK). No prior art or established pattern for multi-signal verdict composition in this codebase is cited, and the plan does not state whether `finalize_verdict` already has an extensible coverage-signal dispatch table or whether the new branch is ad hoc imperative code. If `finalize_verdict` already has a pattern for coverage signals, the new branch may conflict with or duplicate it.
findings.location: Implementation — 'In `orchestrator.finalize_verdict`, deterministic blockers dominate: if blocking is non-empty, verdict remains `BLOCK`; otherwise `coverage.get("prerequisite_indeterminate")` produces `INDETERMINATE`…'
scenarios: If `finalize_verdict` already checks other coverage keys (e.g., a future `parent_indeterminate` or existing keys), the undocumented interaction between them and `prerequisite_indeterminate` can produce unexpected verdicts.
If the existing `finalize_verdict` already has a `coverage`-based INDETERMINATE branch, the new code may duplicate or override it non-obviously.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the `prerequisite-consistency` criterion's preload-conditional activation is expressed in `criteria_routing.json` (e.g., a new `applies_when` key, or a `ProductionBatchRunner` guard that short-circuits before issuing the criterion), and update the routing schema to support it.
evidence: Existing `criteria_routing.json` shows all criteria use `exec`, `facet`, `applies_at`, `block_threshold`, `default_posture`, `checklist` — no `applies_when_preload` or equivalent conditional field exists.
Plan: 'It runs only when the shared `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload succeeded and contains readable direct `depends_on` or inverse `blocks` prerequisites.'

Plan: 'When the criterion is absent it returns an empty coverage array' — but the routing mechanism for this absence is only described as `ProductionBatchRunner` behavior, not as a `criteria_routing.json` field.
The `plan_review_assemble_criteria` op emits `include_prerequisite-consistency: bool`; if the preload fails, this boolean is still `true` unless the routing schema gates it.
findings.finding: The `prerequisite-consistency` criterion registration in `criteria_routing.json` is specified with `default_posture: blocking`, `block_threshold: 0.60`, and `exec: 1-TURN`, but the plan states the criterion 'runs only when the shared `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload succeeded and contains readable direct `depends_on` or inverse `blocks` prerequisites' — a conditional activation that the existing `criteria_routing.json` schema does not support. The existing routing schema (confirmed from `criteria_routing.json` and `registry.py`) has `applies_at.suppress_types`, `applies_at.suppress_when`, and `applies_at.scope` — but no field for 'only when a preload of type X succeeded'. The plan provides no mechanism for the criterion to be absent from the batch when the preload is unavailable, beyond the `ProductionBatchRunner` route — yet the criterion is registered as an LLM 1-TURN criterion and would appear in the `include_prerequisite-consistency` boolean from `plan_review_assemble_criteria`.
findings.location: Binding criterion and CLI registration — 'Register criterion ID `prerequisite-consistency` with `default_posture: blocking`, `block_threshold: 0.60`, and `exec: 1-TURN`'; Implementation — 'It runs only when the shared `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload succeeded'
scenarios: When `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload fails (upstream `75de` not yet deployed), `include_prerequisite-consistency=true` is emitted and the `ProductionBatchRunner` receives a criterion with no preload data, producing undefined behavior not covered by the conditional routing spec.
The `criteria_routing.json` schema has no field to express 'only run when snapshot preload succeeded' — the plan never specifies how to add this conditional or what `applies_at` key covers it.

findings.finding: The plan's mechanism for delivering `relation_snapshot` from the `precheck` step to the `ProductionBatchRunner` via a `with:` key on the `batch:` YAML step does not work: `interpreter.py::_run_batch` never reads `step.get('with')` or `batch.get('with')`, and `BatchRunRequest` has no field for extra with-inputs. The plan states 'precheck exposes the single upstream `relation_snapshot`; finders receives it in `with:` alongside routed criteria' but this delivery channel simply does not exist in the current batch-step infrastructure.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Binding workflow production and verifier batching': 'precheck exposes the single upstream relation_snapshot; finders receives it in with: alongside routed criteria'; also `src/rebar/llm/workflow/interpreter.py::_run_batch` (lines 351-399) and `src/rebar/llm/workflow/runners.py::BatchRunRequest` (lines 61-74)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `BatchRunRequest` with `with_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)` and add to `_run_batch`: `with_inputs = {k: resolve_value(v, rc, prefixes, bindings) for k, v in (step.get('with') or {}).items()}` before building `BatchRunRequest`. Then `ProductionBatchRunner.run(req)` can access `req.with_inputs.get('relation_snapshot')` to retrieve the preload.

findings.finding: The verifier context-limit recovery algorithm is contradicted between two sections: 'Binding focused prompt and failure contracts' specifies splitting the finding-record list into contiguous halves (between PrerequisiteVerificationBlock records), while 'Binding workflow production and verifier batching' specifies that recursive recovery may split an oversized record's finding tuple (within a single PrerequisiteVerificationBlock). These are different splitting strategies for the same scenario and would send an implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Implementation ('Focused verifier context-limit recovery uses the same whole-record algorithm…') vs Binding workflow ('Recursive recovery may split an oversized record's finding tuple, but never splits subject or prerequisite plan text.')

findings.finding: The plan's verdict-mutation step — copying all entries from `parts["blocking"]` to `parts["indeterminate"]` and clearing `parts["blocking"]` when any prerequisite coverage record is indeterminate — suppresses deterministic DET-floor P1/P5/P8 blocking findings because of an unrelated LLM prerequisite-evaluation failure (e.g. a provider timeout or invalid output). DET blocks are structural, model-independent findings; demoting them to INDETERMINATE because a focused LLM call failed conflates two independent failure domains. No rationale is given for why a DET P8-too-big block should be suppressed when a prerequisite snapshot evaluation times out.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'This story explicitly modifies both decision assembly and terminal verdict': 'if any final prerequisite coverage record is indeterminate, copy every entry from `parts["blocking"]` into `parts["indeterminate"]` with `decision=indeterminate` and `reason=prerequisite-coverage-indeterminate`, then clear `parts["blocking"]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Limit the clearing step to non-DET LLM findings: `det_still_blocking = [f for f in parts['blocking'] if f.get('tier') == 'DET']`; move only the LLM-sourced blocking findings to `parts['indeterminate']`; retain `parts['blocking'] = det_still_blocking`. If DET blocks exist, set verdict to BLOCK; if prerequisite is also indeterminate, surface INDETERMINATE as a secondary signal in coverage rather than suppressing the primary BLOCK.

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the behavior when the `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload itself fails (e.g., a transient provider error or network failure before the preload completes). The plan only describes what happens when the preload succeeds with no readable prerequisites (route does not apply → `has_prerequisites=false`, empty arrays) and when individual prerequisite evaluations fail (evaluation-error/output-invalid → indeterminate records). A preload failure is a distinct case: the preload was attempted but errored before producing any snapshot. If this silently maps to `has_prerequisites=false`, a transient upstream failure causes the prerequisite consistency criterion to be skipped entirely without any indeterminate record in the verdict—the review PASSes without having checked prerequisite consistency, and the implementer has no signal that the check was skipped. This is a fail-open gap for a criterion the plan registers as `default_posture: blocking`.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'It runs only when the shared `PlanRelationSnapshot` preload succeeded and contains readable direct `depends_on` or inverse `blocks` prerequisites'; also Upstream contract section; Acceptance Criteria: 'Every readable pinned prerequisite receives exactly one closed-schema disposition'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:484` (the existing `plan_review_decide` `pass3_over_findings` call) as a required edit target to add `execution_review=execution_review`, and add `orchestrator.py` to file_impact for the signature extension.
evidence: Plan: 'Determine execution_review once from the review-phase snapshot supplied by 5967 and pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review) calls'
workflow_ops.py line 484: decided = [*too_big, *shed, *orchestrator.pass3_over_findings(rest, rest_verifs)] — no execution_review
orchestrator.py:678 signature: def pass3_over_findings(findings, verifs) — matches 5967's pre-delivery state
orchestrator.py not in plan file_impact
findings.finding: The plan requires `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` to accept `execution_review=execution_review` (delivered by dep 5967), and states 'pass the same boolean to both general and focused pass3_over_findings(..., execution_review=execution_review) calls.' However, the current `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings` at line 678 has signature `(findings, verifs)` with no `execution_review` parameter. The plan correctly notes this is a 5967 contract, but does not enumerate `orchestrator.py` as an edit target for wiring the existing call sites in `workflow_ops.py` (line 484: `orchestrator.pass3_over_findings(rest, rest_verifs)`) which must also gain the `execution_review` argument.
findings.location: Implementation section + src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:484 + orchestrator.py:678
scenarios: Without updating the existing pass3_over_findings call site in plan_review_decide (workflow_ops.py:484), general findings would not receive the execution floor even after 5967 delivers the capability

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default for any gold sets beyond the named eight (e.g., 'additional sets may be any category; include at least one extra conflict case') so the executor can act without clarification.
evidence: 'Add eight or more gold sets of at least three prerequisites: two all-consistent, two single-conflict, two tail-conflict, one attribution-confusion, and one multi-bin.'
Exactly eight categories are named, so 'or more' has no stated distribution constraint.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'eight or more gold sets' for the prerequisite packing evaluation corpus but provides no guidance on how any gold sets beyond the named eight (two all-consistent, two single-conflict, two tail-conflict, one attribution-confusion, one multi-bin) should be categorized or constructed, leaving the executor to choose arbitrarily.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Add eight or more gold sets of at least three prerequisites...'
scenarios: Executor adds a ninth set of an undecided type and cannot verify whether it satisfies the intent.
A reviewer cannot assess whether the extra sets adequately cover the criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'eight or more' to exactly 'eight' (matching the enumerated categories), or add: 'Additional sets beyond the eight named must each belong to one of the five listed categories, with no category receiving fewer than two sets.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `fidelity_spot_eval.py` already exists and enumerate extending its `__all__` with `prerequisite_packing_spot_eval` and `main` alongside the new CLI additions.
evidence: Plan: 'Add a new module CLI from scratch in fidelity_spot_eval.py using main(argv) plus if __name__ == "__main__": raise SystemExit(main())'

Current fidelity_spot_eval.py __all__ at line 280: ['RELOCATED_PROMPTS', 'relocation_spot_eval', 'packing_spot_eval', 'record_results', 'load_recorded_results', 'recorded_results_path'] — no main or prerequisite_packing_spot_eval
Plan also says 'Add separate opt-in prerequisite_packing_spot_eval' as a new function in this file
findings.finding: The plan names `fidelity_spot_eval.py` as the location for a new module CLI (`main(argv)` plus `if __name__ == '__main__': raise SystemExit(main())`), but the current `fidelity_spot_eval.py` has no `__all__` entry for `main` and no such entry-point. The plan says 'Add a new module CLI from scratch' but the file already exists and its `__all__` would need updating — the `__all__` at line 280 lists existing exports and does not include `main` or `prerequisite_packing_spot_eval`. The plan must enumerate the `__all__` update as an edit.
findings.location: Binding criterion and CLI registration section + src/rebar/llm/plan_review/fidelity_spot_eval.py:280
scenarios: The module entry point `-m rebar.llm.plan_review.fidelity_spot_eval` requires __main__ block; missing __all__ update is a minor gap but indicates the plan treats this as pure creation without noting the pre-existing file's exports need extension

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the `prerequisite_attribution_valid` veto decision rule (`no`/`na` → `attribution-invalid`) in the same Implementation paragraph as the `_build_binary` `extra_fields` signature change, and add a docstring to `plan_review_verification_model` that explicitly states the focused-decide veto semantics for this field.
evidence: Confirmed `_build_binary(forbid: Any) -> type` in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify_models.py` line 91 — no `extra_fields` parameter exists.

Plan: 'Change `_build_binary(forbid, *, extra_fields: dict[str, tuple[type, Any]] | None = None) -> type`; after building existing fields, merge `extra_fields or {}` into the `create_model` field mapping.'

Plan: 'the focused decide path reads `verif["binary"].get("prerequisite_attribution_valid", "na")` as a veto outside validity arithmetic' — this rule is in a different paragraph from the schema change.

Plan: 'Do not add the field to `_BINARY_NA_DEFAULT` or `GRADED_BINARY`' — but this instruction is separate from the schema builder and the veto reader, creating three co-located facts that are split across the Implementation section.
findings.finding: The `prerequisite_attribution_valid` veto rule — 'missing, duplicate, malformed, out-of-range, `no`, or `na` attribution converts that prerequisite record to `indeterminate/attribution-invalid`' — is specified only in the `plan_review_decide` prose section, not co-located with the `_build_binary`/`_build_verification_output` schema change it modifies. The actual `_build_binary` function in `verify_models.py` has signature `_build_binary(forbid: Any) -> type` with no `extra_fields` parameter; the plan proposes changing it to `_build_binary(forbid, *, extra_fields: dict[str, tuple[type, Any]] | None = None)`. But the decision rule that reads `verif["binary"].get("prerequisite_attribution_valid", "na")` as a veto lives in a different section ('the focused decide path reads…'), not in `verify_models.py` or adjacent to the schema. An implementer extending `_build_binary` sees no instruction about the downstream consumer of the new field.
findings.location: Implementation section — `_build_binary`/`_build_verification_output` signature change; Implementation section — 'the focused decide path reads `verif["binary"].get("prerequisite_attribution_valid", "na")` as a veto outside validity arithmetic'
scenarios: An implementer who adds `extra_fields` to `_build_binary` but does not find the veto rule will treat `na` as a valid focused-verifier output and produce blocking focused findings from unvalidated attribution.

The `na`-prohibition for focused entries relies entirely on the verifier prompt instruction ('never `na` for a focused finding') with no schema enforcement — the default `na` from `_build_binary` is a pink-elephant antipattern where the schema invites the model to use a value that the decide layer must then veto.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live-model end-to-end proving command (or an opt-in fidelity eval entry point) that exercises at least one focused verifier call followed by decide integration and asserts the resulting verdict and attribution field, so the verifier→decide→verdict path is covered by a command that cannot pass on mocked substitutes.
evidence: Verification section lists: singleton/baseline fidelity eval (finder only), `pytest` unit tests (mocked), signal-chain tests (fakes supplied per plan: 'supplies a fake focused decide result').
No proving command exercises the path: live-model verifier call → plan_review_decide focused domain → finalize_verdict → BLOCK/INDETERMINATE outcome.
Plan-review.yaml wiring makes the prerequisite path active in production whenever a ticket has `depends_on` relations — it is not behind an opt-in flag in production.
E6-f anti-FP: 'not-applicable when the cutover stays behind a non-default opt-in flag' does not apply here because the production YAML always activates the path for tickets with prerequisites.
findings.finding: The focused Pass-2 verifier + decide integration + verdict assembly path is never exercised end-to-end with a live model in the Verification section. The fidelity eval commands (`--prerequisite-packing --singleton` and `--prerequisite-packing --baseline`) cover only the focused finder; the verifier, decide merging, and terminal verdict steps are validated only by mocked unit tests and faked workflow signal-chain tests.
findings.location: Verification section; Binding implementation sequence steps 1–2; AC: 'Every upheld focused finding appears in the verdict with its authoritative prerequisite ID.'
scenarios: A verifier prompt regression (wrong attribution field name, malformed output schema) passes all mocked tests but fails silently in production, converting valid findings to indeterminate.
A decide-domain merge bug (focused findings shifted into general indices) is invisible until a real two-prerequisite ticket is reviewed live.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal AC covering the `BatchRunRequest.with_inputs` workflow seam: e.g. 'The `with:` YAML key is resolved through the expression resolver and delivered to `ProductionBatchRunner` as `req.with_inputs`; old batch steps without `with:` retain byte-for-byte behavior at the runner boundary.' Add the corresponding seam tests to the Verification test-run commands.
evidence: 'Implement that delivery by extending BatchRunRequest in src/rebar/llm/workflow/runners.py with with_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict), and by updating src/rebar/llm/workflow/interpreter.py::_run_batch to resolve step.get("with", {}) through the normal expression resolver'
ABSENCE: None of the 14 formal AC lines mention BatchRunRequest, with_inputs, interpreter, or the with: YAML seam.
findings.finding: The `BatchRunRequest.with_inputs` seam extension and `interpreter.py` `_run_batch` `with:` resolution are described deliverables with no covering formal AC.
findings.location: Binding workflow production and verifier batching § 'Extend BatchRunRequest … with_inputs'; Acceptance Criteria (none of the 14 AC lines)
scenarios: A reviewer could accept the story as done if all 14 AC pass while the with_inputs seam is implemented incorrectly or incompletely, because no AC gate directly exercises the seam contract.
The seam tests named in the plan (tests/unit/workflow/test_batch_step.py) are also absent from the formal Verification test-run commands, compounding the gap.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
